text: The surgeon begins the procedure by using cold cut dissection to carefully separate adhesions, creating a suitable working space for the robotic-assisted prostatectomy, minimizing the risk of thermal damage to the surrounding intestinal tissue.

The surgical procedure utilizes three robotic arms and a camera controlled by the robot. The assistant operates a workstation to apply suction and traction, and uses staples when necessary. The surgeon manipulates scissors with monopolar energy in the right hand.
The surgeon uses tweezers with bipolar energy in the left hand, while the Prograsp clamp in the third robotic arm assists with tissue manipulation and counter-attraction during the prostatectomy.

The surgeon performs a transperitoneal dissection to address the seminal vesicles, achieving precise visualization and manipulation of these structures. The arteries supplying the seminal vesicles are carefully gripped to minimize cautery use, reducing the risk of damage to nearby neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lateral face of the seminal vesicles, taking care to preserve the surrounding neurovascular structures to maintain erectile function.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles and makes an incision in the fascia of Lennon-Billiers to access the perirectal space, facilitating the separation of the prostate from the rectum. Cold cut dissection is used to minimize rectal injury, while monopolar scissors carefully dissect the anterior wall. The mesh is observed to be close, potentially hindering dissection, but the surgeon navigates through lax tissue until the pubis is visualized, confirming the correct plane and minimizing risk to vascular structures.

The surgeon identifies the endopelvic fascia and begins dissecting the periprostatic fat with precision, ensuring minimal bleeding. This careful dissection aids in the visualization of surrounding tissues and prepares the area for potential histopathological analysis.

The robotic-assisted dissection focuses on the lifting muscles and the dorsal venous complex, preserving a significant amount of muscle fibers. Cold dissection is used to minimize damage to muscle fibers and nerves. The surgeon cuts the puboprostatic ligaments on the left side, enhancing the exposure of the dorsal venous complex.
The surgeon uses robotic needle holders to ligate the dorsal venous complex with a zero PDS suture, employing a running knot technique to achieve a hermetic closure and minimize bleeding.
The surgeon performs a urethropexy as part of the reconstruction process after prostate removal, maintaining the urethra in its original position to potentially aid in recovering continence.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the vesicle neck, introducing electrodes from the Propep system to monitor nearby nerves. The robotic system sends signals to stimulate nerves close to the prostate, with the electrodes capturing graphic waves on the screen, indicating proximity to nerves involved in continence and sexual function.

The surgeon performs the dissection of the vesicle neck, using a technique that involves filling the balloon of the probe and moving it back and forth to identify the interface between the prostate and the bladder, despite unclear dissection planes.

The vesicle neck is opened, and minimal traction is applied to prevent expansion, favoring continence and reducing urinary escape. The surgeon carefully performs posterior dissection in a flat tissue plane, identifying the seminal vesicles while avoiding damage to the trigonum and ureteral meatus.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the posterior vesicle neck, revealing the white and shiny surface of the seminal vesicles in the background. Attention is now directed towards the posterior pedicles of the prostate.

The surgeon begins neuropreservation by performing anterograde and retrograde dissection of the neurovascular bundles surrounding the prostate. Care is taken to minimize tension on the nerves to prevent neuropraxia, which can lead to impotence.

The surgeon performs dissection while maintaining a high position on the neurovascular bundles, using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, occasionally employing cautery. Nerve monitoring is utilized to confirm the correct dissection plane and ensure proper release of the neurovascular bundles.
The surgeon approaches the apex of the prostate, applying cold irrigation to the surgical field to mitigate heat from cauterization and reduce potential nerve damage.
The surgeon performs an additional posterior dissection in the area where the Denonvilliers fascia has been opened, revealing the neurovascular bundles at this level.
The surgeon applies hemolog grafts on the vascular pellicle after releasing the neurovascular axes, achieving early control of bleeding and providing a clear view for continued dissection.

The surgeon continues the dissection using cautery with precision, applying additional grafts to control venous bleeding. Abundant irrigation is used to minimize heat transmission during the procedure.

The surgeon performs dissection and release of the neurovascular axes on the right side, following the same technique as previously described. The right posterior pedicle is also dissected using similar principles, ensuring careful handling of the tissues.

The surgeon completes the apical dissection using cutting and cut-off techniques in the region of the previously placed ligature. The bipolar clamp is used to tighten the tissue, distinguishing it from the prostate. Lax tissue is visible upon entering the space where the urethra is located. Traction is applied to the prostate to enhance visualization of the urethra, while neurovascular bundles are observed in the lower part of the dissection area. The urethra is identified, and efforts are made to preserve the urethral muscle for improved continuity.

After the prostate is removed, the neurovascular bundles are visible on each side of the former prostate location. The urethra is seen with the bundles converging at the midline. Nerve monitoring is used to assess preservation, showing muscle contraction upon nerve stimulation, with a concomitant action potential displayed on a separate screen.

The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the bladder and urethra using two monocryl sutures of different colors, employing the Van Veldhoven technique. The sutures are placed carefully to avoid damage to the urethra and adjacent structures, with precise coordination between the surgeon and assistant.
The surgeon performs a continuous anastomosis between the bladder and urethra, minimizing tissue manipulation and reducing the risk of urinary complications.

The surgeon fills the bladder with at least 120 milliliters of solution to confirm the absence of leakage at the end of the anastomosis. Any identified leakage is addressed to ensure proper healing and reduce the risk of vesicular neck sclerosis. The neurovascular sacs are observed for adequate healing.

The surgeon reduces the pneumoperitoneum to check for bleeding and examines the Douglas bag to ensure there are no liquid collections or unnoticed intestinal injuries at the procedure's initiation site.
